Translation

And now his thunder bolts would Jove wide scatter, but he feared the flames, unnumbered, sacred ether might ignite and burn the axle of the universe: and he remembered in the scroll of fate, there is a time appointed when the sea and earth and Heavens shall melt, and fire destroy the universe of mighty labour wrought. Such weapons by the skill of Cyclops forged, for different punishment he laid aside— for straightway he preferred to overwhelm the mortal race beneath deep waves and storms from every raining sky. And instantly he shut the Northwind in Aeolian caves, and every other wind that might dispel the gathering clouds. He bade the Southwind blow:— the Southwind flies abroad with dripping wings, concealing in the gloom his awful face: the drenching rain descends from his wet beard and hoary locks; dark clouds are on his brows and from his wings and garments drip the dews: his great hands press the overhanging clouds; loudly the thunders roll; the torrents pour; Iris, the messenger of Juno, clad in many coloured raiment, upward draws the steaming moisture to renew the clouds. The standing grain is beaten to the ground, the rustic’s crops are scattered in the mire, and he bewails the long year’s fruitless toil. The wrath of Jove was not content with powers that emanate from Heaven; he brought to aid his azure brother, lord of flowing waves, who called upon the Rivers and the Streams: and when they entered his impearled abode, Neptune, their ancient ruler, thus began; “A long appeal is needless; pour ye forth in rage of power; open up your fountains; rush over obstacles; let every stream pour forth in boundless floods.” Thus he commands, and none dissenting all the River Gods return, and opening up their fountains roll tumultuous to the deep unfruitful sea. And Neptune with his trident smote the Earth, which trembling with unwonted throes heaved up the sources of her waters bare; and through her open plains the rapid rivers rushed resistless, onward bearing the waving grain, the budding groves, the houses, sheep and men,— and holy temples, and their sacred urns. The mansions that remained, resisting vast and total ruin, deepening waves concealed and whelmed their tottering turrets in the flood and whirling gulf. And now one vast expanse, the land and sea were mingled in the waste of endless waves—a sea without a shore. One desperate man seized on the nearest hill; another sitting in his curved boat, plied the long oar where he was wont to plow; another sailed above his grain, above his hidden dwelling; and another hooked a fish that sported in a leafy elm. Perchance an anchor dropped in verdant fields, or curving keels were pushed through tangled vines; and where the gracile goat enjoyed the green, unsightly seals reposed. Beneath the waves were wondering Nereids, viewing cities, groves and houses. Dolphins darting mid the trees, meshed in the twisted branches, beat against the shaken oak trees. There the sheep, affrayed, swim with the frightened wolf, the surging waves float tigers and lions: availeth naught his lightning shock the wild boar, nor avails the stag’s fleet footed speed. The wandering bird, seeking umbrageous groves and hidden vales, with wearied pinion droops into the sea. The waves increasing surge above the hills, and rising waters dash on mountain tops. Myriads by the waves are swept away, and those the waters spare, for lack of food, starvation slowly overcomes at last. A fruitful land and fair but now submerged beneath a wilderness of rising waves, ‘Twixt Oeta and Aonia, Phocis lies, where through the clouds Parnassus’ summits twain point upward to the stars, unmeasured height, save which the rolling billows covered all: there in a small and fragile boat, arrived, Deucalion and the consort of his couch, prepared to worship the Corycian Nymphs, the mountain deities, and Themis kind, who in that age revealed in oracles the voice of fate. As he no other lived so good and just, as she no other feared the Gods. When Jupiter beheld the globe in ruin covered, swept with wasting waves, and when he saw one man of myriads left, one helpless woman left of myriads lone, both innocent and worshiping the Gods, he scattered all the clouds; he blew away the great storms by the cold northwind.

Notes on the passage

First-pass placeholder notes: this page was generated mechanically for coverage and UI review. Replace with passage-specific notes in a later editorial pass.

Preposition

An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

Verb

A word that names an action or a state. A Latin verb inflects for , , , , and . One verb has roughly 150 distinct forms; the four listed in the dictionary entry are enough to generate them all.

First principal part

The first slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular present indicative active (e.g. amo, “I love”). This is the form the lemma is filed under, and it gives the present stem from which all present-system forms are built.

Second principal part

The second slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the present active (e.g. amare, “to love”). It shows which the verb belongs to and how the present system inflects.

Third principal part

The third slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (e.g. amavi, “I have loved”). It gives the perfect stem, from which all forms in the , , and future-perfect active are built.

Fourth principal part

The fourth slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the (e.g. amatum). It gives the stem for the and the future active participle.

Indicative

The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

Present

The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

Active

The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

Perfect

The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

1st-person

Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

Singular

A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

2nd-person

Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3rd-person

Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There’s no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

Plural

A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

Imperative

The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

Adjective

A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

Masculine

One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that’s just a fact about them; it doesn’t always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

Feminine

One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

Neuter

One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

Nominative

The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It’s also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

Genitive

The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl’s book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun’s dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

Dative

The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

Accusative

The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

Ablative

A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

Noun

A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. In Latin, nouns inflect for (six forms — nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, ablative, vocative) and (singular and plural). Every noun belongs to one of five and carries a fixed (masculine, feminine, or neuter), shown in the dictionary entry.

Conjunction

An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

Vocative

The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

Pronoun

A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

Enclitic

A particle attached to the end of the preceding word rather than written separately (-que = “and”; -ne = question marker; -ve = “or”). Primaque in dictionary order is prima + -que.

Principal parts

The set of four verb forms a dictionary entry gives so the reader can build any inflected form. For a regular verb: = the lemma’s dictionary form (1st-person singular present indicative active, e.g. amo); = present active infinitive (amare); = 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (amavi); = supine (amatum).

Infinitive

A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb’s dictionary entry.

Declension

One of five patterns of noun (and adjective) inflection in Latin, identified by the genitive singular ending. 1st declension: -ae (feminine, like forma). 2nd: -i (masculine and neuter). 3rd: -is (all three genders). 4th: -us (mostly masculine). 5th: -ei (mostly feminine).

Pluperfect

The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

Supine

A defective verbal noun in -um (or -u), used in a narrow set of constructions: with verbs of motion to express purpose (venit spectatum, “he came to see”), and as the of a verb’s dictionary entry — the form that gives the stem for the and the future active participle.

Perfect passive participle

The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb’s passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

Gender

The three Latin grammatical genders are , , and . Nouns have a fixed gender; adjectives shift to match the noun they modify.

ab

ab (a, abs) (+ abl.)

from; away from; by (agent); since

Form depends on the following sound: a before consonants, ab before vowels and h, abs (rare) before c, q, t.

que

-que (enclitic conjunction)

and

Attached to the end of the second of the two items being joined. Joins more tightly than et; common in fixed pairs (e.g. senatus populusque).
